figures.caption: Fig. 7: Model performance with different number of nodes used in distributed training for federated learning simulation. Each node has a local copy of the model used for training. Results reported here are for the globally merged inference.

Fig. 8: ROC curve comparing performance on test dataset for models trained on different numbers of features, i.e., 18 and 7 for sub-figures (a) and (b), respectively. Here, the results for federated learning are inferred from a global model with a 3-node setup.

Fig. 2: Projection of a ground state |0⟩ onto the Bloch sphere using (a) only Rx rotations and (b) with a fully entangled layer and universal rotations using Ry and Rz operations. One can see the difference in representation capabilities of the two techniques, where the latter captures more space in this Hilbert space, and hence could be more powerful for certain learning tasks.
Fig. 3: A 3-qubit VQC with angle encoding using Rx rotations and a fully entangled parametrized circuit with Rz and Ry operations. X’s are the input data, whereas θ’s are trainable parameters.
Fig. 5: Federated learning framework for HEP experiments conducted at different colliders.

Fig. 6: Distributed learning setup with a single collider collecting a large amount of data through multiple detectors, and a data distributor which segregates this to multiple nodes (models) for training.
